title: Signing of the Declaration of Independence
clue: Where delegates gathered to sign a document declaring independence from a powerful empire.
description: The Declaration of Independence was signed at Independence Hall in Philadelphia on August 2, 1776, formally announcing the thirteen colonies' separation from Great Britain.
facts: Benjamin Franklin reportedly said, 'We must all hang together, or most assuredly, we shall all hang separately.'
The document was primarily drafted by Thomas Jefferson in just 17 days.

title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
clue: A wall that divided a city for 28 years finally fell, symbolizing the end of the Cold War.
description: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, ending the physical division of East and West Berlin and marking the beginning of German reunification.
facts: The wall stood for 10,316 days.
Over 5,000 people successfully escaped across the wall during its existence.

title: Construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza
clue: The last surviving wonder of the ancient world, built as a tomb for a pharaoh.
description: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built around 2560 BC as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu. It remained the tallest man-made structure for over 3,800 years.
facts: It contains approximately 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing 2.5 tons on average.
The pyramid was originally covered in white limestone casing stones that would have gleamed in the sunlight.

title: Battle of Thermopylae
clue: A narrow coastal pass where 300 warriors made a legendary last stand against a massive invading army.
description: In 480 BC, King Leonidas and 300 Spartans (along with several thousand Greek allies) held the narrow pass of Thermopylae against Xerxes' Persian army.
facts: The Greek force was actually around 7,000 soldiers, not just 300.
The pass was only about 12 meters wide at its narrowest point.

title: Moon Landing — Apollo 11
clue: The launch site from which humanity first left Earth to walk on another celestial body.
description: Apollo 11 launched from Kennedy Space Center on July 16, 1969. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon on July 20.
facts: The Saturn V rocket generated 7.6 million pounds of thrust at liftoff.
Armstrong's first step was watched by an estimated 600 million people worldwide.

title: Signing of the Magna Carta
clue: A meadow where a king was forced to seal a charter limiting royal power, shaping democracy forever.
description: The Magna Carta was sealed by King John at Runnymede in 1215, establishing the principle that everyone, including the king, was subject to the law.
facts: Only 4 of the original 1215 copies survive today.
The charter was originally written in Latin.

title: Bombing of Hiroshima
clue: The first city targeted by a nuclear weapon, forever changing the nature of warfare.
description: On August 6, 1945, the United States dropped the atomic bomb 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima, Japan, killing an estimated 80,000 people instantly.
facts: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial (Genbaku Dome) still stands as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
A bank of oleander flowers was the first thing to bloom after the bombing, becoming the city's official flower.

title: French Revolution — Storming of the Bastille
clue: Revolutionaries stormed a fortress-prison in a capital city, igniting a revolution that reshaped a nation.
description: On July 14, 1789, a mob stormed the Bastille prison in Paris, marking the beginning of the French Revolution and the end of absolute monarchy in France.
facts: Only 7 prisoners were found inside the Bastille when it was stormed.
July 14 is now celebrated as Bastille Day, France's national holiday.

title: Construction of the Colosseum
clue: The largest amphitheater ever built, where gladiators once fought for the entertainment of an empire.
description: The Colosseum in Rome was completed in 80 AD under Emperor Titus. It could hold up to 80,000 spectators and hosted gladiatorial contests for over 400 years.
facts: The Colosseum had a retractable canvas roof called the 'velarium' operated by sailors.
It's estimated that over 400,000 people and 1 million animals died in the arena.

title: D-Day — Normandy Landings
clue: Allied forces launched the largest seaborne invasion in history on these beaches to liberate a continent.
description: On June 6, 1944, over 156,000 Allied troops landed on five beaches along the coast of Normandy, France, beginning the liberation of Western Europe from Nazi control.
facts: The operation involved over 5,000 ships and 13,000 aircraft.
The five beaches were codenamed Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword.

title: Machu Picchu Construction
clue: A hidden citadel built high in the mountains by an empire, abandoned before conquistadors arrived.
description: Machu Picchu was built around 1450 AD as an estate for Inca emperor Pachacuti. It was abandoned during the Spanish Conquest and remained unknown to the outside world until 1911.
facts: The site sits at 2,430 meters above sea level.
No wheels, iron tools, or draft animals were used in its construction.

title: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
clue: An assassination on a city street that triggered a chain of events leading to a world war.
description: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary was assassinated in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, by Gavrilo Princip, directly triggering World War I.
facts: The assassin, Gavrilo Princip, was only 19 years old.
The Archduke's car had taken a wrong turn, placing it directly in front of Princip by chance.

title: Great Wall of China — Major Construction
clue: A massive defensive fortification stretching thousands of miles, begun by the first emperor to unify a nation.
description: Major construction of the Great Wall began under Emperor Qin Shi Huang around 221 BC. The wall ultimately stretched over 13,000 miles across northern China.
facts: The wall is not visible from space with the naked eye, despite the popular myth.
Workers who died during construction were sometimes buried within the wall itself.

title: Titanic Departure
clue: The 'unsinkable' ship departed from this port on its maiden voyage, never to return.
description: RMS Titanic departed from Southampton, England on April 10, 1912, on its maiden voyage to New York City. It struck an iceberg and sank on April 15.
facts: There were only enough lifeboats for about one-third of the passengers.
The ship's band famously continued to play as the ship sank.

title: Nelson Mandela's Imprisonment — Robben Island
clue: An island prison where a future president spent 18 of his 27 years of imprisonment.
description: Nelson Mandela was imprisoned on Robben Island from 1964 to 1982. He spent 27 total years in prison before becoming South Africa's first Black president in 1994.
facts: Mandela's cell was only 8 by 7 feet.
Robben Island is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and museum.

title: Boston Tea Party
clue: Colonists disguised as natives dumped cargo into a harbor to protest taxation without representation.
description: On December 16, 1773, American colonists dressed as Mohawk Indians boarded British ships and dumped 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor.
facts: The tea destroyed was worth about $1.7 million in today's money.
The term 'Boston Tea Party' wasn't used until 50 years after the event.

title: Pompeii Destruction — Eruption of Vesuvius
clue: A Roman city frozen in time by a catastrophic volcanic eruption that buried it under meters of ash.
description: In 79 AD, Mount Vesuvius erupted and buried the Roman city of Pompeii under volcanic ash, preserving it remarkably for nearly 2,000 years.
facts: About 2,000 people died in the eruption out of a population of 20,000.
The city was accidentally rediscovered in 1748 during construction.

title: Wright Brothers' First Flight
clue: Sand dunes and steady winds at this coastal location helped achieve the first powered airplane flight.
description: On December 17, 1903, Orville and Wilbur Wright made the first sustained, controlled, powered heavier-than-air flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
facts: The first flight lasted only 12 seconds and covered 120 feet.
The brothers built their own wind tunnel to test wing designs.

title: Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster
clue: The worst nuclear accident in history occurred at this power plant, creating a permanent exclusion zone.
description: On April 26, 1986, Reactor No. 4 at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in northern Ukraine exploded during a safety test, releasing radioactive contamination across Europe.
facts: The 30-km exclusion zone around Chernobyl has become a wildlife sanctuary.
The reactor was covered with a massive 'New Safe Confinement' structure completed in 2016.

title: Discovery of Tutankhamun's Tomb
clue: Archaeologists discovered an intact pharaoh's tomb in a famous valley, revealing stunning golden treasures.
description: On November 4, 1922, Howard Carter discovered the tomb of Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings, one of the most significant archaeological finds in history.
facts: The tomb contained over 5,000 artifacts, including the iconic gold death mask.
Carter spent 10 years cataloging the contents of the tomb.

title: Taj Mahal Construction
clue: An emperor built this iconic white marble mausoleum as a monument to his beloved wife.
description: The Taj Mahal was commissioned in 1632 by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal. It took about 20 years and 20,000 workers to complete.
facts: The Taj Mahal changes color throughout the day — pinkish in the morning, white during the day, and golden under moonlight.
Over 1,000 elephants were used to transport construction materials.

title: Battle of Gettysburg
clue: The bloodiest battle of the American Civil War, fought in a small Pennsylvania town over three days.
description: The Battle of Gettysburg (July 1-3, 1863) was the turning point of the American Civil War, resulting in over 50,000 combined casualties.
facts: Lincoln's famous Gettysburg Address was only 272 words long.
The battlefield covers over 6,000 acres and contains over 1,300 monuments.

title: Rosetta Stone Discovery
clue: Soldiers found a stone slab in a northern port town that became the key to deciphering ancient hieroglyphics.
description: The Rosetta Stone was discovered in 1799 by French soldiers in the town of Rashid (Rosetta), Egypt. It contained the same text in three scripts, enabling the decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphics.
facts: The stone is actually a fragment of a larger stele.
Jean-François Champollion cracked the hieroglyphic code in 1822 using the stone.

title: Pearl Harbor Attack
clue: A surprise military strike on a naval base drew a nation into the Second World War.
description: On December 7, 1941, Japan launched a surprise attack on the U.S. naval base at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, killing 2,403 Americans and drawing the United States into World War II.
facts: The attack lasted only about 110 minutes.
The USS Arizona memorial sits directly above the sunken battleship, which still leaks oil.

title: Angkor Wat Construction
clue: The world's largest religious monument, originally built as a Hindu temple, later converted to Buddhist use.
description: Angkor Wat was built in the first half of the 12th century by King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire. It is the best-preserved temple at the Angkor archaeological site.
facts: It is the largest religious structure in the world, covering 402 acres.
The temple appears on Cambodia's national flag.

title: Martin Luther's 95 Theses
clue: A monk nailed a list of grievances to a church door, sparking a religious reformation across a continent.
description: On October 31, 1517, Martin Luther reportedly nailed his 95 Theses to the door of All Saints' Church in Wittenberg, Germany, sparking the Protestant Reformation.
facts: Luther originally wrote the theses in Latin, not German.
The church door served as a kind of community bulletin board at the time.

title: Treaty of Versailles Signing
clue: The peace treaty ending the Great War was signed in the Hall of Mirrors at a famous palace.
description: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, at the Palace of Versailles, officially ending World War I and imposing harsh terms on Germany.
facts: The treaty's harsh terms are often cited as a contributing factor to World War II.
The Hall of Mirrors where it was signed contains 357 mirrors.

title: Galileo's Trial by the Inquisition
clue: A scientist faced trial for supporting the theory that the Earth revolves around the Sun.
description: In 1633, Galileo Galilei was tried by the Roman Inquisition in Rome for promoting heliocentrism. He was found guilty and spent the rest of his life under house arrest.
facts: Galileo reportedly muttered 'And yet it moves' after his forced recantation.
The Catholic Church formally apologized to Galileo in 1992.

title: Battle of Waterloo
clue: A decisive battle that ended the rule of a famous French emperor and reshaped European politics.
description: The Battle of Waterloo on June 18, 1815, saw Napoleon Bonaparte defeated by the Duke of Wellington and Prussian forces, ending his reign and the Napoleonic Wars.
facts: The battle lasted about 9 hours.
Napoleon was exiled to the remote island of Saint Helena after the defeat.

title: Petra — Construction of the Treasury
clue: A stunning rock-carved facade in a desert canyon, built by an ancient Arab civilization of traders.
description: Petra, the Nabataean capital in modern-day Jordan, was established around 312 BC. Its most famous structure, Al-Khazneh (The Treasury), was carved directly into sandstone cliffs.
facts: Petra was named one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in 2007.
The city had a sophisticated water management system with dams and cisterns.

title: Russian Revolution — Storming of the Winter Palace
clue: Revolutionaries seized a grand palace, overthrowing a provisional government and establishing communist rule.
description: On October 25-26, 1917, Bolshevik forces stormed the Winter Palace in Petrograd (now St. Petersburg), toppling the Russian Provisional Government.
facts: The actual storming was relatively peaceful, contrary to dramatic movie depictions.
The Winter Palace now houses the Hermitage Museum, one of the world's largest art collections.

title: Panama Canal Opening
clue: A massive engineering feat connecting two oceans through a narrow land bridge between continents.
description: The Panama Canal officially opened on August 15, 1914, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans and revolutionizing global maritime trade.
facts: Over 25,000 workers died during the French and American construction periods, mainly from disease.
Ships are raised 85 feet above sea level through a series of locks.

title: Terracotta Army Discovery
clue: Farmers digging a well accidentally uncovered thousands of life-sized clay soldiers guarding an emperor's tomb.
description: In 1974, farmers near Xi'an, China, discovered the Terracotta Army — over 8,000 unique clay soldiers buried with China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang.
facts: No two warriors have the same face.
The tomb of the emperor himself has never been opened, partly due to legends of mercury rivers inside.

title: Partition of India
clue: The division of a subcontinent into two nations at independence led to one of the largest mass migrations in history.
description: On August 15, 1947, British India was partitioned into India and Pakistan, triggering massive population transfers and communal violence affecting over 15 million people.
facts: An estimated 1-2 million people died in the violence surrounding partition.
The border was drawn by a British lawyer who had never visited India before.

title: Coronation of Charlemagne
clue: A Frankish king was crowned emperor by the Pope on Christmas Day, reviving the idea of a Western Roman Empire.
description: On December 25, 800 AD, Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne as Emperor of the Romans at St. Peter's Basilica in Rome, establishing the Holy Roman Empire.
facts: Charlemagne could speak Latin and Greek but reportedly could not write.
His empire covered most of Western Europe.

title: Cuban Missile Crisis
clue: A 13-day confrontation between superpowers over nuclear missiles placed on a Caribbean island.
description: In October 1962, the Soviet Union placed nuclear missiles in Cuba, bringing the U.S. and USSR to the brink of nuclear war. The crisis was resolved through diplomacy.
facts: Soviet submarine officer Vasili Arkhipov may have single-handedly prevented nuclear war by refusing to launch a torpedo.
The crisis lasted exactly 13 days, from October 16-28.

title: Acropolis of Athens — Construction of the Parthenon
clue: A marble temple dedicated to a goddess of wisdom, built atop a hill overlooking a famous ancient city-state.
description: The Parthenon was built between 447-432 BC on the Athenian Acropolis, dedicated to the goddess Athena. It is considered the epitome of Classical Greek architecture.
facts: The Parthenon has no straight lines — the columns lean slightly inward and the base curves upward.
Lord Elgin controversially removed many of its marble sculptures in the early 1800s.

title: Lewis and Clark Expedition Start
clue: An expedition departed from a frontier town near two great rivers to explore newly purchased western territory.
description: The Lewis and Clark Expedition departed from Camp Dubois near St. Louis on May 14, 1804, to explore the newly acquired Louisiana Purchase territory and find a route to the Pacific.
facts: The expedition covered approximately 8,000 miles over 2.5 years.
Sacagawea, a Shoshone woman, served as interpreter and guide for much of the journey.

title: Tiananmen Square Protests
clue: Pro-democracy protesters gathered in a massive public square before a military crackdown shocked the world.
description: In June 1989, pro-democracy protests in Beijing's Tiananmen Square were violently suppressed by the Chinese military. The iconic 'Tank Man' image became a global symbol of resistance.
facts: The protests lasted about 7 weeks before the crackdown.
The exact number of casualties remains unknown and is still censored in China.

title: Abolition of Slavery — Emancipation Proclamation
clue: A president issued a proclamation from the executive mansion declaring enslaved people in rebel states to be free.
description: On January 1, 1863, President Abraham Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation from the White House, declaring all enslaved people in Confederate states to be free.
facts: The Proclamation only freed enslaved people in Confederate states, not border states.
It transformed the Civil War into a fight for human liberty, not just preservation of the Union.

title: Stonehenge Construction
clue: A mysterious circle of massive standing stones on a plain, aligned with the solstice sunrise.
description: Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain, England, was built in stages from around 3000 BC. Its purpose remains debated, but it aligns precisely with the summer solstice sunrise.
facts: Some stones were transported over 150 miles from Wales.
The largest stones weigh about 25 tons each.

title: Opening of the Suez Canal
clue: A canal was built through a desert isthmus, connecting two seas and revolutionizing global shipping routes.
description: The Suez Canal opened on November 17, 1869, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea and eliminating the need to sail around Africa.
facts: The canal is about 120 miles long and has no locks.
An estimated 1.5 million workers were involved in the construction, with thousands dying.

title: Aztec Capital Tenochtitlan
clue: An empire built its capital city on an island in a lake, with causeways and canals rivaling European cities.
description: Tenochtitlan was founded in 1325 on an island in Lake Texcoco, becoming the capital of the Aztec Empire. It was one of the largest cities in the world with a population of over 200,000.
facts: Modern Mexico City is built directly on top of Tenochtitlan's ruins.
The city featured a complex system of aqueducts and floating gardens called chinampas.

title: Signing of the Treaty of Waitangi
clue: A treaty between indigenous chiefs and a colonial power that became the founding document of a Pacific island nation.
description: The Treaty of Waitangi was signed on February 6, 1840, between the British Crown and Maori chiefs in New Zealand, establishing British law while protecting Maori rights.
facts: There are significant differences between the English and Maori versions of the treaty.
Waitangi Day on February 6 is New Zealand's national day.

title: Building of the Hagia Sophia
clue: An emperor built the world's largest cathedral at the crossroads of Europe and Asia, featuring a revolutionary dome.
description: The Hagia Sophia was built in Constantinople (Istanbul) by Emperor Justinian I, completed in 537 AD. It served as a cathedral, mosque, museum, and is now a mosque again.
facts: Its massive dome spans 31 meters and seemed to 'float' above the building.
It remained the world's largest cathedral for nearly 1,000 years.

title: Gold Rush — Sutter's Mill Discovery
clue: Gold was found at a sawmill, triggering a massive migration of prospectors to the American West.
description: On January 24, 1848, James W. Marshall discovered gold at Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California, sparking the California Gold Rush that drew 300,000 people west.
facts: The Gold Rush increased California's population from about 14,000 to 300,000 in just four years.
John Sutter, who owned the mill, actually went bankrupt because of the Gold Rush.

title: Mayan City of Chichen Itza — El Castillo
clue: An ancient pyramid in the jungle designed so that a serpent shadow appears during the equinox.
description: El Castillo (Temple of Kukulcán) at Chichen Itza was built around 600 AD by the Maya civilization. During equinoxes, shadows create a serpent pattern descending the stairs.
facts: The pyramid has exactly 365 steps, one for each day of the year.
Clapping at the base produces an echo that sounds like a quetzal bird.

title: Galileo's Telescope Observations in Padua
clue: An astronomer pointed a new instrument at the sky from this Italian university city and changed our understanding of the cosmos.
description: In 1610, Galileo Galilei made groundbreaking astronomical observations from Padua, Italy, discovering Jupiter's moons and the phases of Venus.
facts: Galileo's telescope had only 20x magnification, less than modern binoculars.
He named Jupiter's four large moons the 'Medicean Stars' to honor his patrons.

title: Zulu Victory at Battle of Isandlwana
clue: A major African military force defeated a powerful European colonial army in a stunning upset during the Anglo-Zulu War.
description: On January 22, 1879, Zulu warriors overwhelmed a British force at Isandlwana in South Africa, inflicting the worst defeat by indigenous forces on the British Empire.
facts: Over 1,300 British soldiers were killed, while the Zulus lost about 1,000.
The Zulu army used a tactical formation called the 'horns of the buffalo.'

title: Silk Road — Ancient Trade Route
clue: An ancient network of trade routes began from this imperial capital, connecting East and West for centuries.
description: The Silk Road trade network was established around 130 BC, with Xi'an (then Chang'an) as its eastern terminus, connecting China to the Mediterranean world.
facts: The name 'Silk Road' was coined by German geographer Ferdinand von Richthofen in 1877.
Besides silk, goods like spices, gold, glass, and ideas traveled along the routes.

title: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' Speech
clue: A civil rights leader delivered a famous speech before thousands gathered at a national monument.
description: On August 28, 1963, Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech from the steps of the Lincoln Memorial during the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.
facts: Over 250,000 people attended the March on Washington.
The 'I Have a Dream' portion was partly improvised, encouraged by gospel singer Mahalia Jackson.

title: Eruption of Mount Vesuvius (Pompeii)
clue: A catastrophic volcanic eruption buried entire Roman cities under ash, preserving them for millennia.
description: Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD, burying the Roman cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum. The eruption lasted about 24 hours and released thermal energy far exceeding the Hiroshima bombing.
facts: Pliny the Younger wrote the only surviving eyewitness account of the eruption.
The type of eruption is now called 'Plinian' after Pliny the Elder, who died investigating it.

title: First Olympic Games
clue: The first organized athletic competition in the Western world was held at a sacred site in ancient Greece.
description: The first recorded Olympic Games were held in 776 BC at Olympia, Greece. They were dedicated to Zeus and held every four years for over 1,000 years.
facts: The only event in the first Olympics was a 192-meter sprint called the 'stadion.'
A sacred truce was called during the games, halting all wars between Greek city-states.

title: Assassination of Julius Caesar
clue: A dictator was stabbed to death by senators on the Ides of March in a famous political assassination.
description: On March 15, 44 BC, Julius Caesar was assassinated by a group of Roman senators, including Brutus and Cassius, at the Theatre of Pompey in Rome.
facts: Caesar was stabbed 23 times.
His last words to Brutus are debated — Shakespeare's 'Et tu, Brute?' is likely dramatic invention.

title: Founding of Constantinople
clue: A Roman emperor founded a new capital city at a strategic strait between two continents.
description: Emperor Constantine I founded Constantinople (modern Istanbul) in 330 AD as the new capital of the Roman Empire, strategically located on the Bosphorus strait.
facts: The city was protected by massive walls that withstood sieges for over 1,000 years.
Constantinople was the largest and wealthiest city in Europe for most of the Middle Ages.

title: Rwandan Genocide
clue: A devastating genocide occurred in this small African nation over approximately 100 days.
description: Between April and July 1994, an estimated 800,000 Rwandans, primarily Tutsis and moderate Hutus, were killed in one of the fastest genocides in history.
facts: The killing lasted approximately 100 days.
The international community has been widely criticized for failing to intervene.

title: Marco Polo Arrives in China
clue: A Venetian merchant arrived at the court of a Mongol emperor after a years-long journey across Asia.
description: Marco Polo reached the court of Kublai Khan in Beijing around 1275, beginning a 17-year stay in China. His later accounts introduced Europeans to Asian civilization.
facts: Some historians question whether Polo actually visited China, as he never mentioned the Great Wall or tea.
His book 'The Travels of Marco Polo' inspired Christopher Columbus.

title: First Transatlantic Flight — Charles Lindbergh
clue: An aviator departed from a New York airfield for a solo nonstop flight across the Atlantic Ocean.
description: On May 20, 1927, Charles Lindbergh took off from Roosevelt Field on Long Island in the Spirit of St. Louis, completing the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight.
facts: The flight took 33.5 hours, covering 3,610 miles.
Lindbergh stayed awake for 55 hours straight during the preparation and flight.

title: Viking Settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows
clue: Norse explorers established the first known European settlement in the Americas, centuries before Columbus.
description: Around 1000 AD, Vikings led by Leif Erikson established a settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, Canada — the earliest known European settlement in the Americas.
facts: The site was discovered in 1960 by Norwegian explorers.
It predates Columbus's arrival by nearly 500 years.

title: Apartheid Begins in South Africa
clue: A system of institutionalized racial segregation was formally established when a new government took power in this city.
description: In 1948, the National Party won elections in South Africa and began implementing apartheid — a system of institutionalized racial segregation that lasted until 1994.
facts: The word 'apartheid' means 'separateness' in Afrikaans.
South Africa was banned from the Olympic Games from 1964 to 1992 due to apartheid.

title: Battle of the Somme
clue: One of the bloodiest battles of World War I, fought along a river in northern France over five months.
description: The Battle of the Somme (July–November 1916) was one of the largest battles of WWI. On the first day alone, the British suffered 57,470 casualties.
facts: Tanks were used in combat for the first time during this battle.
Over 1 million men were killed or wounded on all sides.

title: Battle of Stalingrad
clue: A brutal urban siege on the banks of the Volga became the turning point of World War II's Eastern Front.
description: The Battle of Stalingrad (August 1942–February 1943) was one of the deadliest battles in history. The Soviet victory marked the turning point of WWII in Europe.
facts: An estimated 2 million people were killed, wounded, or captured.
The city is now called Volgograd.

title: Battle of Midway
clue: A decisive naval battle near a Pacific atoll turned the tide of war in the Pacific Theater.
description: The Battle of Midway (June 1942) was a decisive American naval victory that destroyed four Japanese aircraft carriers and shifted the balance of power in the Pacific.
facts: American codebreakers had deciphered Japanese plans before the battle.
Japan lost four fleet carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu.

title: Battle of Britain
clue: Fighter pilots defended their island nation from sustained aerial bombardment during World War II.
description: The Battle of Britain (July–October 1940) was the first major military campaign fought entirely by air forces. The RAF's defense prevented a German invasion of Britain.
facts: Churchill said, 'Never in the field of human conflict was so much owed by so many to so few.'
Radar technology gave Britain a crucial advantage.

title: Dunkirk Evacuation
clue: Hundreds of thousands of trapped Allied soldiers were rescued from beaches across the English Channel by a fleet of civilian boats.
description: The Dunkirk evacuation (May–June 1940) rescued over 338,000 Allied soldiers from the beaches of Dunkirk, France, using military and civilian vessels.
facts: Over 800 civilian 'little ships' participated in the rescue.
Churchill called it a 'miracle of deliverance' but warned 'wars are not won by evacuations.'

title: Nuremberg Trials
clue: Major war criminals faced justice in a German city's courtroom after World War II.
description: The Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946) prosecuted prominent leaders of Nazi Germany for war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide, establishing precedents for international law.
facts: Twelve defendants were sentenced to death by hanging.
Hermann Göring committed suicide the night before his scheduled execution.

title: Bombing of Nagasaki
clue: The second atomic bomb was dropped on this Japanese city, three days after the first.
description: On August 9, 1945, the United States dropped the 'Fat Man' atomic bomb on Nagasaki, killing approximately 40,000 people instantly. Japan surrendered six days later.
facts: Nagasaki was actually the secondary target — the primary target, Kokura, was obscured by clouds.
The bomb was a plutonium implosion device, different from the uranium bomb used on Hiroshima.

title: V-E Day — German Surrender at Reims
clue: Germany signed its unconditional surrender in a schoolhouse in this French city, ending WWII in Europe.
description: On May 7, 1945, General Alfred Jodl signed Germany's unconditional surrender at SHAEF headquarters in Reims, France, ending World War II in Europe.
facts: The surrender was signed in a red schoolhouse that served as Eisenhower's headquarters.
The Soviet Union insisted on a second signing ceremony in Berlin the next day.

title: Gallipoli Campaign
clue: Allied forces attempted an amphibious landing on a peninsula to knock an empire out of World War I.
description: The Gallipoli Campaign (1915–1916) was a failed Allied attempt to control the Dardanelles strait. The defeat shaped the national identities of Australia, New Zealand, and Turkey.
facts: ANZAC Day (April 25) commemorates the landing of Australian and New Zealand forces.
Mustafa Kemal (later Atatürk) gained fame defending the peninsula.

title: Iwo Jima — Raising the Flag
clue: Marines raised a flag atop a volcanic island, creating one of the most iconic photographs of World War II.
description: On February 23, 1945, U.S. Marines raised the American flag on Mount Suribachi during the Battle of Iwo Jima. The photograph became an enduring symbol of WWII.
facts: Three of the six flag raisers in the famous photo were killed in subsequent fighting on the island.
The battle lasted 36 days and cost nearly 7,000 American lives.

title: Napoleon's Exile to Elba
clue: A defeated emperor was exiled to a small Mediterranean island, only to escape and reclaim power briefly.
description: After his abdication in April 1814, Napoleon was exiled to the island of Elba off the coast of Italy. He escaped in February 1815, returning to power for the Hundred Days.
facts: Napoleon was given sovereignty over Elba and allowed to keep 600 soldiers.
The famous palindrome 'Able was I ere I saw Elba' was likely invented later.

title: Gandhi's Salt March
clue: A leader marched 240 miles to the sea to protest a colonial tax on a common mineral, sparking a movement.
description: Mahatma Gandhi led the Salt March from his ashram to the coastal town of Dandi in 1930, defying British salt taxes in an act of nonviolent civil disobedience.
facts: The march covered 240 miles over 24 days.
Over 80,000 Indians were jailed for making or selling salt in defiance of the law.

title: Assassination of Abraham Lincoln
clue: A president was shot while watching a play at a theater in the nation's capital.
description: On April 14, 1865, President Abraham Lincoln was shot by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C. He died the following morning.
facts: Lincoln was watching 'Our American Cousin' when he was shot.
Booth broke his leg jumping from the presidential box to the stage.

title: Assassination of JFK
clue: A president was shot while riding in a motorcade through a Texas city.
description: President John F. Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963, in Dallas, Texas, while riding in an open motorcade through Dealey Plaza.
facts: Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested for the assassination but was killed two days later by Jack Ruby.
The Zapruder film captured the assassination on 8mm home movie film.

title: Churchill's 'We Shall Fight on the Beaches' Speech
clue: A wartime leader rallied his nation with a famous speech delivered in the houses of parliament.
description: On June 4, 1940, Winston Churchill delivered his 'We shall fight on the beaches' speech to the House of Commons, rallying British resolve after the Dunkirk evacuation.
facts: Churchill gave three major speeches in June 1940, collectively his most famous oratory.
The speech was not broadcast live — Churchill re-recorded it for radio years later.

title: Alexander the Great at the Battle of Gaugamela
clue: A young Macedonian king decisively defeated the Persian Empire on the plains of ancient Mesopotamia.
description: In 331 BC, Alexander the Great defeated Darius III at Gaugamela (near modern Mosul, Iraq), effectively ending the Persian Achaemenid Empire.
facts: Alexander was only 25 years old at the time of the battle.
Despite being heavily outnumbered, Alexander's superior tactics won the day.

title: Genghis Khan Conquers Beijing
clue: A Mongol conqueror captured the capital of a Chinese dynasty, expanding the largest land empire in history.
description: In 1215, Genghis Khan's Mongol forces captured Zhongdu (modern Beijing), the capital of the Jin dynasty, as part of his campaign that created the vast Mongol Empire.
facts: The Mongol Empire eventually stretched from Korea to Eastern Europe.
Genghis Khan's actual burial site remains unknown to this day.

title: Cleopatra's Death in Alexandria
clue: The last pharaoh of an ancient kingdom died in her palace in a great Mediterranean port city.
description: Cleopatra VII, the last active ruler of Ptolemaic Egypt, died in Alexandria in 30 BC, allegedly by snakebite, after the defeat of her forces by Octavian (Augustus).
facts: Cleopatra spoke nine languages and was the first Ptolemaic ruler to learn Egyptian.
Her death marked the end of the Ptolemaic Kingdom and Egypt's annexation by Rome.

title: Columbus Lands in the Americas
clue: An Italian explorer sailing for Spain made landfall on a Caribbean island, believing he had reached Asia.
description: On October 12, 1492, Christopher Columbus landed on San Salvador in the Bahamas, marking the beginning of sustained European contact with the Americas.
facts: Columbus made four voyages to the Americas but never realized he hadn't reached Asia.
He initially called the indigenous people 'Indians' because he thought he was in the Indies.

title: Vasco da Gama Reaches India
clue: A Portuguese explorer arrived at this Indian port city, completing the first sea route from Europe to Asia.
description: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut (Kozhikode), India, on May 20, 1498, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to Asia around the Cape of Good Hope.
facts: The voyage took about 10 months from Lisbon.
The sea route broke the monopoly of Arab and Venetian traders on the spice trade.

title: Darwin's Arrival at the Galápagos Islands
clue: A naturalist arrived at volcanic islands in the Pacific, where unique wildlife would inspire a revolutionary theory.
description: Charles Darwin visited the Galápagos Islands in 1835 aboard HMS Beagle. His observations of finches and tortoises contributed to his theory of evolution by natural selection.
facts: Darwin spent only five weeks in the Galápagos.
He didn't fully develop his theory of natural selection until years after the voyage.

title: Discovery of Penicillin
clue: A scientist accidentally discovered the world's first antibiotic in his London laboratory.
description: In 1928, Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin at St. Mary's Hospital in London when he noticed mold killing bacteria in a petri dish, revolutionizing medicine.
facts: Fleming's discovery was largely accidental — he left a petri dish uncovered before going on vacation.
Penicillin saved millions of lives during World War II.

title: Marie Curie's Radioactivity Research
clue: A scientist discovered two radioactive elements in a laboratory in the French capital.
description: In 1898, Marie Curie and her husband Pierre discovered polonium and radium at the School of Physics and Chemistry in Paris, pioneering the study of radioactivity.
facts: Curie was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize and the only person to win in two different sciences.
Her laboratory notebooks are still too radioactive to handle without protection.

title: First Successful Heart Transplant
clue: A surgeon performed the world's first human heart transplant at a hospital in a South African city.
description: On December 3, 1967, Dr. Christiaan Barnard performed the first successful human heart transplant at Groote Schuur Hospital in Cape Town, South Africa.
facts: The patient, Louis Washkansky, survived 18 days before dying of pneumonia.
Barnard's second transplant patient lived over 19 months.

title: Krakatoa Eruption
clue: A volcanic island between two larger islands erupted with one of the loudest sounds in recorded history.
description: The 1883 eruption of Krakatoa in Indonesia was one of the most catastrophic volcanic events in history, killing over 36,000 people and triggering massive tsunamis.
facts: The explosion was heard nearly 3,000 miles away in Australia.
The eruption caused global temperatures to drop by 1.2°C the following year.

title: Cortés Arrives in the Aztec Capital
clue: A Spanish conquistador entered a magnificent island city and was received by its emperor.
description: Hernán Cortés arrived in Tenochtitlan on November 8, 1519, and was received by Emperor Montezuma II. Within two years, the Aztec Empire would fall to the Spanish.
facts: The Spanish were astonished by Tenochtitlan, which was larger than any European city at the time.
Smallpox brought by the Spanish devastated the Aztec population.

title: Korean War — Battle of Inchon
clue: A daring amphibious assault behind enemy lines turned the tide of a war on the Korean Peninsula.
description: On September 15, 1950, General MacArthur launched a surprise amphibious landing at Inchon, South Korea, cutting off North Korean supply lines and recapturing Seoul.
facts: The landing was considered extremely risky due to extreme tidal variations at Inchon.
Within two weeks, Seoul was recaptured from North Korean forces.

title: Vietnam War — Fall of Saigon
clue: A helicopter evacuation from an embassy rooftop marked the end of a long and divisive war.
description: On April 30, 1975, Saigon fell to North Vietnamese forces, ending the Vietnam War. The chaotic helicopter evacuation from the U.S. Embassy became an iconic image.
facts: Saigon was renamed Ho Chi Minh City after the fall.
Over 7,000 people were evacuated by helicopter in the final 24 hours.

title: Construction of the Statue of Liberty
clue: A gift from one nation to another, this copper colossus greeted immigrants arriving in a famous harbor.
description: The Statue of Liberty was dedicated on October 28, 1886, as a gift from France to the United States, becoming a symbol of freedom and immigration.
facts: The statue was designed by Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi, with its iron framework built by Gustave Eiffel.
The statue's full name is 'Liberty Enlightening the World.'

title: Bastogne — Battle of the Bulge
clue: Surrounded American forces held a critical crossroads town during Germany's last major offensive in WWII.
description: During the Battle of the Bulge (December 1944), the 101st Airborne Division held Bastogne, Belgium, against a German siege. General McAuliffe famously replied 'Nuts!' to a German surrender demand.
facts: The Battle of the Bulge was the deadliest battle for American forces in WWII.
Patton's Third Army relieved Bastogne after a remarkable forced march.

title: Signing of the Japanese Surrender
clue: Japan formally surrendered aboard a battleship anchored in a bay, ending World War II.
description: On September 2, 1945, Japan signed the instrument of surrender aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, officially ending World War II.
facts: The ceremony lasted about 23 minutes.
General Douglas MacArthur used six pens during the signing.

title: Destruction of the Library of Alexandria
clue: The ancient world's greatest repository of knowledge, located in a Mediterranean port city, was gradually destroyed.
description: The Library of Alexandria, founded around 283 BC, was the largest and most significant library of the ancient world. Its destruction, occurring in stages, resulted in an incalculable loss of knowledge.
facts: The library may have held between 40,000 and 400,000 scrolls.
Multiple events contributed to its destruction, including fires during Julius Caesar's siege.

title: Hannibal Crosses the Alps
clue: A Carthaginian general led an army with war elephants across a mountain range to invade the Roman Republic.
description: In 218 BC, Hannibal Barca crossed the Alps with approximately 30,000 soldiers and 37 war elephants to invade Italy, one of the most audacious military feats in history.
facts: Hannibal lost nearly half his army during the treacherous Alpine crossing.
He remained in Italy for 15 years, winning major battles but never capturing Rome.

title: Sinking of the Spanish Armada
clue: A powerful naval fleet was defeated in the English Channel, ending one empire's dominance of the seas.
description: In 1588, the Spanish Armada — a fleet of 130 ships — was defeated by the English navy and devastated by storms, marking the decline of Spanish naval supremacy.
facts: The English used fire ships to scatter the Armada at the Battle of Gravelines.
Only about half of the Spanish ships made it back to Spain.

title: Construction of the Eiffel Tower
clue: An iron lattice tower was built for a world's fair and became the most iconic landmark of a European capital.
description: The Eiffel Tower was built for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris, designed by Gustave Eiffel. Originally criticized by many, it became the world's most visited paid monument.
facts: The tower was originally intended to stand for only 20 years.
It was the world's tallest structure until the Chrysler Building was completed in 1930.

title: Execution of Marie Antoinette
clue: A queen was executed by guillotine in a public square during the Reign of Terror.
description: Marie Antoinette, Queen of France, was executed by guillotine at the Place de la Révolution (now Place de la Concorde) in Paris on October 16, 1793.
facts: She likely never said 'Let them eat cake' — the phrase predates her.
Her last words were reportedly an apology to the executioner for stepping on his foot.

title: Discovery of Machu Picchu by Hiram Bingham
clue: An American explorer rediscovered a hidden Inca citadel high in the Andes, overgrown by jungle.
description: On July 24, 1911, Hiram Bingham III was guided to Machu Picchu by local farmers. Though known to locals, his expedition brought it to international attention.
facts: Bingham was actually searching for the lost city of Vilcabamba, not Machu Picchu.
He removed thousands of artifacts, which Peru successfully campaigned to have returned from Yale.

title: Construction of the Great Sphinx
clue: A colossal limestone statue with a human head and lion's body guards a pharaoh's pyramid complex.
description: The Great Sphinx of Giza was carved from a single mass of limestone around 2500 BC, likely during the reign of Pharaoh Khafre. It is the oldest known monumental sculpture.
facts: The Sphinx's nose is missing — it may have been chiseled off in the 14th century.
The statue was buried up to its shoulders in sand for much of its history.

title: Fall of Constantinople
clue: Ottoman forces breached the legendary walls of a great city, ending over a thousand years of a Christian empire.
description: On May 29, 1453, Sultan Mehmed II conquered Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire after 1,123 years and establishing the Ottoman Empire's capital.
facts: The Ottomans dragged ships overland to bypass the chain blocking the Golden Horn harbor.
The fall is often cited as the end of the Middle Ages.

title: Bolívar Liberates Bogotá
clue: A revolutionary leader crossed the Andes and liberated a colonial capital, earning the title 'The Liberator.'
description: Simón Bolívar led his army across the Andes and defeated the Spanish at the Battle of Boyacá on August 7, 1819, liberating Bogotá and establishing Gran Colombia.
facts: Bolívar's crossing of the Andes is compared to Hannibal's crossing of the Alps.
He liberated six nations: Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Panama, and Bolivia.

title: Meiji Restoration
clue: An emperor reclaimed power from a feudal military government, rapidly modernizing an island nation.
description: The Meiji Restoration of 1868 ended the Tokugawa shogunate and restored imperial rule under Emperor Meiji, transforming Japan from a feudal society into a modern industrial state.
facts: Japan went from a feudal society to a major world power in just 50 years.
The capital was moved from Kyoto to Edo, renamed Tokyo.

title: Signing of the Camp David Accords
clue: Leaders of two Middle Eastern nations signed a historic peace agreement at a presidential retreat in the mountains.
description: In September 1978, Egyptian President Sadat and Israeli Prime Minister Begin signed the Camp David Accords, brokered by President Carter, leading to the first Arab-Israeli peace treaty.
facts: Sadat and Begin shared the 1978 Nobel Peace Prize.
Sadat was assassinated in 1981, partly because of the peace deal with Israel.

title: Construction of the Forbidden City
clue: An emperor built a vast palace complex of nearly a thousand buildings in the heart of his capital.
description: The Forbidden City in Beijing was completed in 1420 under the Yongle Emperor of the Ming dynasty. It served as the imperial palace for 500 years and contains 980 buildings.
facts: It is the largest ancient palatial structure in the world, covering 180 acres.
Commoners were forbidden from entering, hence its name.

title: Tesla and Westinghouse Power Niagara Falls
clue: The first large-scale alternating current power plant harnessed the energy of a massive waterfall.
description: In 1896, the Niagara Falls Power Company began transmitting electricity to Buffalo, NY, using Nikola Tesla's AC system, proving alternating current's superiority for power distribution.
facts: The project settled the 'War of Currents' between Tesla's AC and Edison's DC system.
Power was transmitted 26 miles to Buffalo — a remarkable distance at the time.

title: Fidel Castro's Cuban Revolution
clue: Guerrilla fighters marched into a Caribbean capital after overthrowing a dictator, establishing a communist state.
description: On January 1, 1959, Fidel Castro's revolutionary forces took control of Havana after Fulgencio Batista fled Cuba, establishing a communist government 90 miles from the United States.
facts: Castro initially denied being a communist, only declaring Cuba socialist in 1961.
He remained in power for 49 years, making him one of the longest-serving heads of state.

title: 9/11 — Attack on the World Trade Center
clue: Terrorist attacks destroyed twin skyscrapers in a major American city, reshaping global politics.
description: On September 11, 2001, terrorists hijacked commercial airliners and crashed them into the World Trade Center in New York City, killing 2,977 people in the deadliest attack on U.S. soil.
facts: The Twin Towers collapsed within 102 minutes of being struck.
The 9/11 Memorial features the largest man-made waterfalls in North America.

title: Nelson's Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar
clue: A British admiral won a decisive naval battle off the coast of Spain but was killed during the fighting.
description: On October 21, 1805, Admiral Horatio Nelson defeated the combined French and Spanish fleets at the Battle of Trafalgar, securing British naval supremacy for a century.
facts: Nelson was killed by a French sniper during the battle.
His body was preserved in a barrel of brandy for the voyage home.

title: Boxer Rebellion — Siege of the Legations
clue: Foreign embassies in an imperial capital were besieged for 55 days by a nationalist uprising.
description: In 1900, the Boxer Rebellion saw Chinese nationalists besiege the foreign legation quarter in Beijing for 55 days before an international relief force arrived.
facts: The Boxers were officially called the 'Society of Righteous and Harmonious Fists.'
Eight nations sent forces to relieve the siege.

title: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Dedication
clue: A memorial museum opened in a city devastated by atomic warfare to promote world peace.
description: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum opened in 1955, built near the hypocenter of the 1945 atomic bombing, to convey the reality of nuclear weapons and promote peace.
facts: The Genbaku Dome, left standing as a ruin, became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996.
Over 1.5 million people visit the memorial annually.

title: Suez Crisis
clue: A president nationalized a critical waterway, sparking an international crisis involving three invading nations.
description: In 1956, Egypt's President Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal, prompting a military intervention by Britain, France, and Israel. International pressure forced their withdrawal.
facts: The crisis is considered the end of Britain and France as major global powers.
The U.S. and Soviet Union both opposed the invasion, a rare Cold War agreement.

title: Einstein Publishes Special Relativity in Bern
clue: A patent clerk in a Swiss city published papers that revolutionized physics, including E=mc².
description: In 1905, while working as a patent clerk in Bern, Switzerland, Albert Einstein published four groundbreaking papers, including his theory of special relativity and the equation E=mc².
facts: 1905 is known as Einstein's 'Annus Mirabilis' or 'miracle year.'
He was only 26 years old and had been rejected for academic positions.

title: Magellan's Expedition Reaches the Philippines
clue: A Portuguese explorer leading a Spanish fleet was killed in a battle on a tropical island during the first circumnavigation.
description: Ferdinand Magellan was killed at the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines on April 27, 1521. His remaining crew completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth.
facts: Only 18 of the original 270 crew members completed the voyage.
Magellan himself never completed the circumnavigation due to his death.

title: The Blitz — Bombing of London
clue: Sustained aerial bombing campaigns targeted a European capital for months, but its citizens refused to surrender.
description: The Blitz (September 1940–May 1941) saw Germany bomb London for 57 consecutive nights, killing over 30,000 civilians. Londoners famously sheltered in Underground stations.
facts: Over a million London houses were destroyed or damaged.
St. Paul's Cathedral survived largely intact, becoming a symbol of London's resilience.

title: Construction of Christ the Redeemer
clue: A massive statue of a religious figure with outstretched arms was completed atop a mountain overlooking a coastal city.
description: Christ the Redeemer was completed in 1931 atop Corcovado mountain in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. The 98-foot Art Deco statue became one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.
facts: The statue was built with reinforced concrete and soapstone.
It is struck by lightning an average of 3-5 times per year.

title: Yalta Conference
clue: Three Allied leaders met at a palace on the Black Sea coast to plan the post-war division of Europe.
description: In February 1945, Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin met at Yalta in Crimea to discuss the reorganization of post-war Europe, decisions that shaped the Cold War.
facts: FDR died just two months after the conference.
The agreements at Yalta effectively divided Europe into Western and Soviet spheres of influence.

title: Tokugawa Shogunate Established
clue: A military ruler unified a war-torn island nation, establishing a shogunate that would bring 250 years of peace.
description: Tokugawa Ieyasu established the Tokugawa Shogunate in 1603 after winning the Battle of Sekigahara, beginning 250 years of peace and isolation known as the Edo period.
facts: Japan's policy of 'sakoku' (closed country) severely restricted foreign trade and travel.
The Tokugawa period saw the flourishing of kabuki theater, ukiyo-e art, and haiku poetry.

title: Attack on Fort Sumter
clue: The first shots of a civil war were fired at a coastal fort in a southern harbor.
description: On April 12, 1861, Confederate forces fired on Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor, South Carolina, beginning the American Civil War.
facts: No one was killed during the 34-hour bombardment.
Major Robert Anderson surrendered the fort on April 14, four years to the day before Lincoln's assassination.

title: Printing Press Invented by Gutenberg
clue: A goldsmith in a German city invented a device that revolutionized the spread of knowledge across Europe.
description: Around 1440, Johannes Gutenberg developed the movable-type printing press in Mainz, Germany, enabling mass production of books and transforming European society.
facts: The Gutenberg Bible was the first major book printed with movable type.
Only 49 copies of the Gutenberg Bible are known to survive.

title: Israeli Declaration of Independence
clue: A new nation declared independence in a museum on a boulevard in a Mediterranean coastal city.
description: On May 14, 1948, David Ben-Gurion proclaimed the State of Israel at the Tel Aviv Museum, establishing the first Jewish state in nearly 2,000 years.
facts: Five Arab nations invaded the new state the very next day.
The declaration ceremony lasted only 32 minutes.

title: Great Fire of London
clue: A fire that started in a bakery destroyed most of a major European city over four days.
description: The Great Fire of London began on September 2, 1666, in a bakery on Pudding Lane. It destroyed 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and much of the medieval City of London.
facts: Despite the massive destruction, only six verified deaths were recorded.
Sir Christopher Wren redesigned 51 churches and St. Paul's Cathedral after the fire.
